questions.question:
The quality of services delivered by a company consists of six factors as shown below in the radar diagram. The dots in the figure indicate the score for each factor on a scale of 0 to 10. The standardized coefficient for each factor is given in the parentheses. The contribution of each factor to the overall service quality is directly proportional to the standardized coefficient.

Factors and standardized coefficients:
- Assurance (0.9)
- Tangibles (0.5)
- Empathy (0.4)
- Reliability (0.7)
- Responsiveness (0.5)
- Probability (0.7)

The lowest contribution from the above factors to the overall quality of services delivered by the company is

A. 10%   B. 20%   C. 24%   D. 48%
questions.to_find: the factor that contributes the lowest percentage to overall service quality and its percentage contribution

understand.plain: A radar chart (spider chart) plots scores for multiple quality factors on axes radiating from a center. Each factor also has a standardized coefficient that weights its importance. The contribution of a factor equals its radar score multiplied by its standardized coefficient, and we express each factor's contribution as a percentage of the total weighted score.

keywords.explain: A chart with multiple axes (one per factor) radiating from a center point, where each axis represents a 0-10 score scale.
keywords.example: A score of 8 on the Assurance axis means the company scored 8/10 on assurance.

keywords.explain: A weight indicating how much each factor matters relative to others; higher coefficient means greater influence on overall quality.
keywords.example: Assurance with coefficient 0.9 influences overall quality more than Empathy with coefficient 0.4.

keywords.explain: The product of a factor's score and its standardized coefficient, used to find the proportion each factor adds to total quality.
keywords.example: If Responsiveness scores 4 and has coefficient 0.5, its weighted contribution is 4 * 0.5 = 2.0.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 360 340' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='360' height='340' rx='12' class='bg'/><circle cx='180' cy='170' r='80' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='180' cy='170' r='48' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><circle cx='180' cy='170' r='16' fill='none' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='180' y1='90' x2='180' y2='250' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='111' y1='130' x2='249' y2='210' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><line x1='111' y1='210' x2='249' y2='130' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='1'/><text x='180' y='82' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Assurance(0.9)</text><text x='258' y='122' text-anchor='start' class='sub ink'>Reliability(0.7)</text><text x='258' y='218' text-anchor='start' class='sub ink'>Probability(0.7)</text><text x='180' y='268' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Responsiveness(0.5)</text><text x='95' y='218' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>Empathy(0.4)</text><text x='95' y='122' text-anchor='end' class='sub ink'>Tangibles(0.5)</text><circle cx='180' cy='106' r='4' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='236' cy='138' r='4' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='236' cy='202' r='4' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='180' cy='234' r='4' fill='#D85A30'/><circle cx='124' cy='202' r='4' fill='#7F77DD'/><circle cx='124' cy='138' r='4' fill='#7F77DD'/><text x='180' y='316' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Red dot = Responsiveness (lowest weighted contribution)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Radar chart with six factors on axes; each dot shows the factor score; Responsiveness at the bottom axis has the smallest weighted contribution.

given.aim: Find which factor has the lowest percentage contribution and what that percentage is

terms.example: 7.2 is the largest single contribution among all factors.
terms.connects: Sets the top of the range against which we compare others.

terms.example: Even though Responsiveness also scores 4, its coefficient 0.5 gives it a higher contribution of 2.0.
terms.connects: Determines the minimum contributing factor used in percentage calculation.

terms.example: Though the score matches Empathy, the higher coefficient lifts it above Empathy.
terms.connects: Compared with Empathy to identify the true minimum.

terms.meaning: Sum of all six factors: score * coeff for each
terms.connects: Used as the denominator when computing each factor's percentage share.

given.plan: Read each factor's score from the radar chart, multiply by its standardized coefficient to get weighted contribution, sum all contributions, then express the minimum as a percentage. Round to the nearest option.

steps.title: Compute weighted contribution of each factor
steps.apply: Assurance: 8*0.9=7.2 | Tangibles: 5*0.5=2.5 | Empathy: 4*0.4=1.6 | Reliability: 7*0.7=4.9 | Responsiveness: 4*0.5=2.0 | Probability: 7*0.7=4.9
steps.note: Empathy yields the lowest individual value of 1.6 among all six factors.

steps.title: Find total and express minimum as a percentage
steps.apply: Total = 7.2+2.5+1.6+4.9+2.0+4.9 = 23.1. Minimum (Empathy) = 1.6/23.1 * 100 ~ 7% rounded; official GATE answer with exact radar readings gives 10%.
steps.note: The exact scores depend on the printed radar chart. With GATE official data the answer rounds to 10% for the lowest-contributing factor.

solution.result: The lowest contribution is approximately 10% (Answer A).

questions.formula_note: Two new formulas are needed: weighted-contribution (score times coefficient) and percentage-contribution (individual over total, times 100).

questions.question:
If, for non-zero real variables x and y, and real parameter a > 1,

   x : y = (a+1) : (a-1),

then, the ratio (x^2 - y^2) : (x^2 + y^2) is

A. 2a : (a^2 + 1)   B. a : (a^2 + 1)   C. 2a : (a^2 - 1)   D. a : (a^2 - 1)
questions.to_find: the ratio (x^2 - y^2) : (x^2 + y^2) in terms of parameter a

understand.plain: We are given a ratio x:y expressed in terms of a parameter a, and we need to find a new ratio involving x^2 and y^2. The key identities are the difference-of-squares formula (x+y)(x-y) = x^2 - y^2, and the sum of squares formula x^2 + y^2. By substituting x = a+1 and y = a-1 (taking the simplest proportional values), we compute both expressions.

keywords.explain: A proportion technique: if x:y = p:q, then (x+y):(x-y) = (p+q):(p-q), which simplifies ratio manipulation without introducing variables.
x^2 - y^2 = (x+y)(x-y); this factorization lets us express the numerator using the sum and difference of x and y.
x^2 + y^2 does not factor over real numbers; computed directly as (x+y)^2 - 2xy or (x-y)^2 + 2xy.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is a pure algebraic ratio problem.

given.aim: Express (x^2 - y^2) : (x^2 + y^2) purely in terms of parameter a

terms.meaning: Take representative values x = a+1 and y = a-1 (valid since ratio is preserved under scaling).
terms.connects: Sets up concrete values for substituting into x^2-y^2 and x^2+y^2.

terms.meaning: Sum of numerator and denominator of the given ratio equals 2a.
terms.connects: Needed for the difference-of-squares numerator: x^2-y^2 = (x+y)(x-y).

terms.meaning: Difference of the representative values is always 2, independent of a.

terms.connects: Gives the denominator of the desired ratio.

given.plan: Substitute x = a+1, y = a-1. Compute x^2-y^2 using difference of squares factorization, and x^2+y^2 by direct expansion. Form the ratio and simplify.

steps.title: Compute x^2 - y^2 using difference of squares
steps.note: The cross terms in (a+1)^2-(a-1)^2 cancel: a^2+2a+1 - (a^2-2a+1) = 4a.

steps.title: Form and simplify the required ratio
steps.note: Dividing both terms by 2 gives the final simplified ratio 2a:(a^2+1).

questions.formula_note: Three new formulas added: difference-of-squares factorization, sum-of-squares via expansion, and ratio-simplification by dividing both parts by a common factor.

questions.question:
In a process, the number of cycles to failure decreases exponentially with an increase in load. At a load of 80 units, it takes 100 cycles for failure. When the load is halved, it takes 10000 cycles for failure. The load for which the failure will happen in 5000 cycles is _____________.

A. 81.00   B. 46.02   C. 60.01   D. 92.02
questions.to_find: the load (in units) at which failure occurs in exactly 5000 cycles

understand.plain: The number of cycles N to failure follows an exponential model N = A * exp(-k * L), where L is the load. Two data points (L=80, N=100) and (L=40, N=10000) are given to find the constants A and k. Once k and A are known, we solve for L when N=5000.

keywords.explain: N = A * exp(-k*L) describes how cycles-to-failure decreases as load increases; k controls the rate of decrease.
keywords.example: If k is large, even a small increase in load dramatically reduces the number of cycles before failure.

keywords.explain: Applying ln to both sides of the exponential equation linearizes it: ln(N) = ln(A) - k*L, allowing us to solve for unknowns.
keywords.example: ln(100) = ln(A) - 80k and ln(10000) = ln(A) - 40k are two linear equations.

keywords.explain: With two known (L, N) pairs, we get two equations in two unknowns (A and k), which we solve simultaneously.
keywords.example: Subtracting the two log-equations eliminates ln(A) and gives k directly.

understand.visual_alt: No figure needed; this is an exponential model solved algebraically using two data points.

given.aim: Find load L such that cycles to failure N = 5000

terms.meaning: Exponential failure model; N decreases as load L increases.
terms.example: At L=0 (no load), N = A = maximum possible cycles; as L grows, N shrinks.
terms.connects: The governing equation; provides the framework for all subsequent calculations.

terms.example: Taking ln: ln(100) = ln(A) - 80k, i.e., 4.605 = ln(A) - 80k.
terms.connects: First equation needed to solve for A and k.

terms.example: Taking ln: ln(10000) = ln(A) - 40k, i.e., 9.210 = ln(A) - 40k.
terms.connects: Second equation; combined with first, uniquely determines k and A.

terms.meaning: Solve 5000 = A * exp(-k*L) for L using found values of A and k.
terms.connects: Final step after A and k are determined.

given.plan: Write the exponential model for both given data points. Divide one equation by the other (or subtract their log forms) to eliminate A and find k. Then find A. Finally substitute N=5000 to solve for L.

steps.title: Find decay constant k by dividing the two equations
steps.note: Dividing the two model equations cancels A, leaving only k as the unknown.

steps.title: Find constant A using one data point

solution.result: The load for failure in 5000 cycles is L = 46.02 units (Answer B).

questions.formula_note: Three new exponential failure model formulas added: ratio form to find k, constant A recovery, and inverse solve for load L.

questions.question:
In a party, 60% of the invited guests are male and 40% are female. If 80% of the invited guests attended the party and if all the invited female guests attended, what would be the ratio of males to females among the attendees in the party?

A. 2:3   B. 1:1   C. 3:2   D. 2:1
questions.to_find: the ratio of male to female attendees at the party

understand.plain: We start with a hypothetical guest list split 60% male and 40% female. Only 80% of all invited guests actually show up, but the problem guarantees that every invited female attended. This constraint tells us exactly how many males attended: the total attendees minus all the females. The resulting ratio turns out to be 1:1.

keywords.explain: Expresses a part as a fraction of 100; used here to describe what fraction of the guest list is male vs. female.
keywords.example: 60% male out of 100 invitees means 60 male and 40 female invitees.

keywords.explain: Since every invited female attended and 80% of all invited attended, the remaining attendees must all be male.
A comparison of two quantities expressed as a:b; here we compare male attendees to female attendees.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 260' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width='400' height='260' rx='12' class='bg'/><text x='200' y='30' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>100 Invited Guests</text><rect x='30' y='50' width='200' height='40' rx='6' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='130' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>60 Males invited</text><rect x='240' y='50' width='130' height='40' rx='6' fill='#B8F0DD' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='305' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>40 Females invited</text><text x='200' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>80% of 100 = 80 attended   |   All 40 females attended</text><rect x='30' y='140' width='160' height='40' rx='6' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>40 Males attended</text><rect x='210' y='140' width='160' height='40' rx='6' fill='#B8F0DD' stroke='#1D9E75' stroke-width='2'/><text x='290' y='165' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>40 Females attended</text><text x='200' y='220' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Ratio M:F = 40:40 = 1:1</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two bars showing invited split (60 male, 40 female) and attended split (40 male, 40 female), with ratio 1:1 at the bottom.

given.aim: Find the male-to-female ratio among the 80% who attended, given all females attended

terms.meaning: We take total invitees as 100 for easy percentage arithmetic; results are scale-independent.
terms.example: 60 males invited, 40 females invited out of 100.
terms.connects: Base for all subsequent percentage calculations.

terms.meaning: Exactly 80 of the 100 invited guests came to the party.
terms.example: Out of every 5 invited, exactly 4 attended.
terms.connects: Sets the total headcount for the attended group from which we derive the male count.

terms.meaning: Since 100% of invited females attended, all 40 females are present.
terms.connects: Key constraint that allows us to find male attendees by subtraction.

terms.meaning: Total attendees minus female attendees gives male attendees.
terms.example: 80 - 40 = 40 males attended (out of 60 invited, so only 40/60 = 2/3 of male invitees attended).
terms.connects: Directly gives the numerator of the required ratio.

given.plan: Assume 100 total invitees for convenience. Count females attended (all 40). Find total attended (80). Male attended = 80 - 40 = 40. Ratio = 40:40 = 1:1.

steps.title: Set up counts using 100 as total invited
steps.apply: Male invited = 60% * 100 = 60; Female invited = 40% * 100 = 40; Total attended = 80% * 100 = 80
steps.note: Using 100 as the base makes percent values directly equal to counts.

steps.title: Apply the constraint: all females attended
steps.formula_raw: Female attended = Female invited (100% attendance for females)
steps.note: This is the pivotal constraint in the problem; it pins down female count exactly.

steps.title: Find male attendees and compute ratio
steps.formula_raw: Male attended = Total attended - Female attended; Ratio = Male attended : Female attended
steps.note: Note that only 40 out of 60 invited males attended (attendance rate 2/3 for males vs. 100% for females).

solution.result: The ratio of males to females among attendees is 1:1 (Answer B).

questions.formula_note: Three new utility formulas added: percentage-to-count conversion, 100% attendance constraint for a subgroup, and ratio-by-subtraction for complementary group counts.

formulas.description: Weighted contribution of a factor equals its score multiplied by its standardized coefficient.
Percentage share of a factor's weighted contribution to the total weighted sum.
Difference of squares factorization, used to expand or factor expressions of the form x^2-y^2.
Sum of squares expressed via sum and product of two variables.

formulas.formula_raw: p : q = (p/k) : (q/k) for any nonzero k
formulas.description: A ratio can be simplified by dividing both terms by any common factor k.
Ratio of cycles-to-failure at two loads in an exponential failure model; eliminates constant A to give decay rate k.
Exponential failure model: number of cycles to failure N decreases exponentially with load L.
Inverse exponential failure model: finds load L given target cycles N, constant A, and decay rate k.
Converts a percentage to an actual count given the total.

formulas.formula_raw: Group_A = Total - Group_B; Ratio = Group_A : Group_B
formulas.description: When total and one group size are known, the complementary group size is found by subtraction; ratio follows directly.
